fact: September 4 is exactly 247 days into the year - that's more than two-thirds of the whole year gone!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many spiders start spinning their webs like crazy because they're getting ready to lay eggs before winter hits.
The sun is setting about 2-3 minutes earlier each day around September 4, which means daylight is disappearing faster than a melting ice cream cone.
Maple trees in cold regions start pumping sugar down to their roots right around this time, which is why their leaves will turn those amazing red and orange colors soon.
September 4 falls during Virgo season in the zodiac calendar.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 4 is when spring flowers like wattle trees explode with bright yellow blooms that smell like honey.
The morning dew gets heavier around this date in many places because the air temperature and ground temperature start having bigger differences.
Monarch butterflies in North America are in the middle of their epic 3,000-mile journey to Mexico - some might be flying right over your head!
September 4 is only about 18 days away from the autumn equinox, when day and night become exactly the same length everywhere on Earth.
Squirrels go absolutely nuts around this time - they can bury up to 25 acorns per day and remember where most of them are hidden.
The moon phase on September 4 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, you can see way more stars because there's no moonlight washing them out.
In northern places like Canada, the first frost can happen around this time, turning spider webs into glittery crystal decorations in the morning.
